paper_title: TOBench: A Task-Oriented Omni-Modal Benchmark for Real-World Tool-Using Agents

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Tool-using agents are increasingly expected to operate across realistic professional workflows, where they must interpret multimodal inputs, coordinate external tools, inspect intermediate artifacts, and revise their actions before producing a final result. Existing benchmarks, however, often evaluate tool use, computer use, and multimodal reasoning in isolation, leaving a gap between benchmark settings and end-to-end omni-modal tool use in the real world. To address this gap, we introduce MM-ToolBench, a benchmark and evaluation harness for task-oriented omni-modal tool use. MM-ToolBench contains 100 executable tasks from two macro task families, Customer Service and Intelligent Creation, covering 20 subcategory slices and supported by 27 MCP servers with 324 tools. The central design of MM-ToolBench is closed-loop multimodal verification: agents must execute tools, inspect rendered or transformed artifacts, and self-correct when outputs fail task-specific requirements. To make such evaluation scalable and verifiable, MM-ToolBench couples MCP-based execution with task-specific grounded evaluators and a semi-automated construction pipeline for scenario discovery, task instantiation, evaluator synthesis, and human audit. Experiments on 15 contemporary agentic models show that MM-ToolBench remains highly challenging: Claude Opus 4.6, commonly regarded as one of the strongest coding-agent models, achieves only 32.0% task success, far below the 94.0% human benchmark. We envision MM-ToolBench as a practical foundation for evaluating and advancing next-generation omni-modal tool-using agents through closed-loop multimodal verification.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Tool-using agents are increasingly expected to operate across realistic professional workﬂows, where they must interpret multimodal inputs, coordinate external tools, inspect intermediate artifacts, and revise their actions before producing ﬁnal result. Existing benchmarks, however, often evaluate tool use, computer use, and multimodal reasoning in isolation, leaving gap between benchmark settings and end-to-end omni-modal tool use in the real world. To address this gap, we introduce TOBench, benchmark and evaluation harness for task-oriented omni-modal tool use. TOBench contains 100 executable tasks from two macro task families, Customer Service and Intelligent Creation, covering 20 subcategory slices and supported by 27 MCP servers with 324 tools. The central design of TOBench is closed-loop multimodal veriﬁcation: agents must execute tools, inspect rendered or transformed artifacts, and self-correct when outputs fail task-speciﬁc requirements. To make such evaluation scalable and veriﬁable, TOBench couples MCPbased execution with task-speciﬁc grounded evaluators and semi-automated construction pipeline for scenario discovery, task instantiation, evaluator synthesis, and human audit. Experiments on 15 contemporary agentic models show that TOBench remains highly challenging: Claude Opus 4.6, commonly regarded as one of the strongest coding-agent models, achieves only 32.0% task success, far below the 94.0% human benchmark. We envision TOBench as practical foundation for evaluating and advancing next-generation omni-modal tool-using agents through closed-loop multimodal veriﬁcation.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Tool-using language agents, powered by foundation models such as GPT-4 [1] and GPT-4o [2], are moving from isolated function invocation toward practical interaction with web services, ofﬁce software, knowledge sources, and external applications. The Model Context Protocol (MCP) further accelerates this shift by providing standard interface for connecting agents to diverse tools and services. As result, recent benchmarks have made substantial progress in evaluating API use, function calling, planning, and MCP-based tool interaction, including τ -bench [3], ToolBench [4], BFCL [5], ToolTalk [6], Toolathlon [7], MCP-RADAR [8], MCP-Bench [9], and MCP-Universe [10]. Despite this progress, existing benchmarks still leave critical gap for real-world professional workﬂows. Many practical tasks are not purely textual or purely API-based: an agent may need to read screenshots or documents, extract information from audio or video, edit spreadsheet or presentation, render the output, inspect whether the result satisﬁes visual and semantic constraints, and then Equal Contribution. Corresponding author. Preprint. Figure 1: Performance of leading LLMs on TOBench. Qwen 3.5 Plus achieves the highest model score of 41.0%, while most other models fall below 30.0%. The human benchmark is 94.0%, highlighting signiﬁcant performance gap and indicating substantial room for improvement in real-world multimodal tool-invocation tasks. revise the artifact if necessary. This diversity gap goes beyond adding more tool names or longer tool lists. The difﬁculty lies in coordinating tool execution with multimodal perception, artifact transformation, and iterative veriﬁcation over changing workspace states. Multimodal and computer-use benchmarks such as OSWorld [11], VitaBench [12], M3-Bench [13], and OmniGAIA [14] broaden evaluation beyond text, but multimodal perception and tool use are still often evaluated as separate capabilities. Tool-use benchmarks typically emphasize schema ﬁdelity, tool selection, or ﬁnal-state checking, while multimodal benchmarks often focus on perception, GUI control, or ﬁnal-answer quality. Realistic omni-modal workﬂows require all of these capabilities simultaneously: agents must perceive heterogeneous inputs, act through executable tools, inspect intermediate artifacts, and self-correct under task-speciﬁc constraints. To address this gap, we introduce TOBench, benchmark and evaluation harness for task-oriented omni-modal tool use. TOBench contains 100 executable tasks across two macro task families, Customer Service and Intelligent Creation, covering 20 subcategory slices and supported by 27 MCP servers with 324 tools. Tasks are designed around realistic user needs and professional roles rather than synthetic tool combinations, and many of them require cross-tool composition over documents, images, audio, video, spreadsheets, slides, search, browser automation, and ﬁle operations. The central design of TOBench is closed-loop multimodal veriﬁcation. Instead of treating tool use as one-shot action sequence followed by ﬁnal-answer matching, TOBench requires agents to execute tools, inspect rendered or transformed artifacts, and revise their behavior when the artifact does not satisfy the task. Each task is paired with grounded veriﬁer that combines code-based checks, toolcall constraints, format constraints, and multimodal artifact inspection. This makes the benchmark an executable harness for evaluating the full perceiveactinspectrevise loop. Experiments on 15 contemporary agentic models show that TOBench is far from saturated. As shown in Figure 1, the strongest evaluated model, Qwen3.5-Plus, achieves only 41.0% task success, while the human benchmark reaches 94.0%. Our failure analysis shows that errors concentrate in unreliable tool execution, incorrect tool parameters, multimodal reasoning failures, and missing self-veriﬁcation before stopping. These results suggest that closed-loop multimodal veriﬁcation is an indispensable evaluation primitive for next-generation omni-modal tool-using agents.

sections.title: 2.2 Multimodal and Computer-Use Agent Benchmarks
sections.content: OSWorld, AndroidWorld, VisualWebArena, VitaBench, τ -Voice, MMDR-Bench, VisualAgentBench, ProSoftArena, M3-Bench, Tool-LMM, UniVA, and OmniGAIA broaden evaluation toward GUI grounding and multimodal interaction [11, 23, 24, 12, 2528, 13, 29, 30, 14, 3135]. TOBench is closest to this line, but differs in three ways: it targets realistic professional task completion, uses uniﬁed MCP-based tool ecosystem, and centers evaluation on iterative artifact inspection with taskspeciﬁc grounded veriﬁers. Table 1 summarizes this comparison from the perspective of benchmark scale, ecosystem assumptions, and multimodal execution requirements.

sections.title: 3 TOBench
sections.content: TOBench evaluates whether an agent can complete realistic omni-modal tasks with executable tools. Each task instance speciﬁes the user instruction, task assets, available tool environment, and grounded veriﬁer used to determine success. Together, these components deﬁne professional role, multimodal inputs, an executable tool ecosystem, and task-speciﬁc veriﬁcation path.

sections.title: 3.2 TOBench Construction Framework
sections.content: Figure 2 illustrates the overall construction pipeline of TOBench. We build tasks from realistic professional scenarios by selecting omni-modal MCP tools, discovering user-centered scenarios, instantiating executable tasks, and curating multimodal assets for closed-loop veriﬁcation. 4 Figure 3: Distribution of tools, task difﬁculty, and task scenarios in TOBench. Left: the number of calls for each tool, showing long-tailed tool-usage distribution. Middle: the distribution of task difﬁculty levels. Right: the scenario distribution of tasks across two high-level categories, Custom Service and Intelligent Creation. Omni MCP Tool Selection. We extend the Toolathlon [7] MCP stack toward omni-modal workﬂows. We retain broadly useful tools for browser automation, retrieval, ofﬁce editing, ﬁlesystems, and search, and add multimodal servers for PPT editing, text-to-speech, speech recognition, and video or audio processing. We also implement two benchmark-speciﬁc servers, Image Generation Server and Image Processing Toolkits, to support creation tasks and closed-loop visual inspection. The ﬁnal benchmark integrates 27 MCP servers and 324 tools in total. This diversity is necessary because many tasks require cross-tool composition rather than single API. The full inventory appears in Appendix A. Omni-modal Scenario Discovery. We begin from realistic user needs rather than synthetic tool combinations. Our scenario-discovery prompt takes category, subcategory, and the available MCP servers as input, and asks language model to produce 10 candidate scenarios in JSON format. Each candidate contains scenario name, vivid description that couples user need with an appropriate agent role, and candidate MCP set. The prompt explicitly enforces four constraints that mirror our design goals: (1) each scenario must be expressed as user need + agent role, (2) multimodal evidence must arise naturally in the input, (3) the required workﬂow must be feasible under the provided tools, and (4) the scenario should rely on simple and commonly veriﬁable domain rules rather than niche expert knowledge. The prompt also prefers image-based inputs over unnecessarily long videos unless temporal information is essential, which improves realism and keeps benchmark execution efﬁcient. Across 20 subcategories, this process yields roughly 200 candidate scenarios in total. Omni-modal Benchmark Task Instantiation Given discovered scenario, we instantiate executable tasks through structured task-generation prompt framed as useragent role-play. Each generated task is serialized as ﬁxed JSON object containing task_name, task_difficulty, turn_mode, required_mcp, agent_config, user_request, and input_files, which makes the result directly runnable and auditable. The prompt requires the user request to remain natural and free of tool-name leakage, while the agent is assigned professional role with concise but veriﬁable domain rules, as elaborated in Appendix . Difﬁculty is controlled primarily by requirement complexity, ambiguity, and workﬂow length rather than by artiﬁcially large assets. The prompt further enforces tool feasibility, everyday realism, resource efﬁciency, and ﬂexible single-turn or multi-turn interaction, followed by ﬁnal reﬂection step that revises unsupported or incomplete tasks before they are admitted into the benchmark. For each scenario, we generate three task candidates corresponding to easy, medium, and hard difﬁculty levels, yielding roughly 600 task candidates overall. Multimodal Asset Curation. We favor compact but information-dense multimodal artifacts. In line with the prompts above, images are used whenever they are sufﬁcient, while video or audio is reserved for cases in which temporal reasoning is genuinely necessary. Assets may come from public web content or controlled generation pipelines when needed, and we normalize them for privacy, reproducibility, and practical execution cost at benchmark scale. Asset curation required 5 Figure 4: Overview of the evaluation pipeline in TOBench. Rather than using one benchmark-wide evaluator, we generate and audit task-speciﬁc evaluation points and task-speciﬁc evaluation code, and then execute the resulting evaluator under time-sensitive, grounded conditions. substantial manual effort: two AI PhD students spent approximately one month collecting realistic cases and corresponding input ﬁles from real-world workﬂows. During this process, we ﬁltered out scenarios that were unrealistic, weakly grounded, or difﬁcult to support with suitable input artifacts. In total, roughly two-thirds of the initially collected cases were discarded, leaving about 200 highquality cases for subsequent task instantiation and benchmark construction. Since some MCP tools did not provide sufﬁciently reliable execution capabilities to support task completion, our ﬁnal benchmark contains 100 tasks organized into two macro families: Customer Service (67 tasks): service-oriented scenarios such as education, e-commerce, government services, medicine, insurance, technical support, and travel. Intelligent Creation (33 tasks): artifact-creation scenarios such as ofﬁce editing, advertising, social content, game assets, and design-oriented workﬂows. These two macro categories cover two major application spaces for agentic systems. We further instantiate 20 subcategory slices in total. Figure 3 summarizes the taxonomy.

sections.title: 4 Evaluation Harness
sections.content: In many TOBench tasks, correctness depends on output structure, multimodal content, role-speciﬁc constraints, intermediate tool usage, and externally grounded information. Final success therefore cannot be reduced to string matching or single software-state check. In TOBench, evaluation is constructed as task-level harness: each task binds an executable environment to grounded veriﬁer. Figure 4 summarizes the pipeline. For task i, we organize its approved evaluation criteria as Cjudge (8) corresponding to format constraints, judge-based multimodal constraints, and tool/result constraints. sections.title: 4.1 Task-Speciﬁc Evaluation Point Generation
sections.content: The ﬁrst stage generates task-speciﬁc evaluation points from the user request, agent role, domain rules, expected outputs, and ground-truth workspace. Rather than using one rubric for the whole 6 benchmark, we derive Ci separately for each task. The resulting points fall into three categories: format constraints, judge-based multimodal constraints, and tool/result constraints  (Table 5)  . Because TOBench contains heterogeneous and partially open-ended tasks, all generated evaluation points are manually reviewed to remove omissions, unsupported assumptions, and duplicate checks. 4.2 Task-Speciﬁc Grounded Evaluator Synthesis and Human Audit We then generate grounded evaluation script for each task rather than applying single benchmarkwide evaluator. The synthesized code implements Vi by combining deterministic checks, VLMbased judging [36], and tool-aware veriﬁcation over MCP logs or live external results. Shared utilities handle common operations such as spreadsheet parsing, document rendering, image conversion, and judge invocation, while task-speciﬁc logic is specialized per criterion. Each evaluator is manually audited before use. Representative prompts and reference evaluator code will be released with the benchmark pipeline. 4.3 Execution-Time Evaluation and Timeliness TOBench adopts task-level success as the primary metric: task is counted as solved only when all relevant evaluation points pass. If task has mi approved evaluation points with binary outcomes ei,j {0, 1}, we deﬁne task success as mi Si = ei,j, (10) j=1 so task passes only when every required criterion passes. Documents may need to be rendered into images before visual inspection; audio outputs may need transcription; spreadsheets and ofﬁce ﬁles may require structured parsing; and some criteria require re-querying MCP tools or checking tool-call traces to conﬁrm that the agent relied on grounded results rather than unsupported generation. This execution-time veriﬁer is what makes TOBench harness rather than static answer set.Execution-time validation is critical for time-sensitive benchmark tasks involving live data such as search, maps, weather, ﬁnance, or changing web content. Evaluators should run soon after task completion to avoid external changes corrupting ground truth. Unlike static ﬁle checks, tool-result checks may re-run MCP queries or inspect tool logs.

sections.title: 5.1 Experimental Setting
sections.content: We evaluate TOBench on all 100 tasks, spanning 67 Customer Service tasks and 33 Intelligent Creation tasks, with easy/medium/hard splits. Each task exposes only its relevant subset of MCP servers and common utilities, and each run is capped at 100 interaction turns. Table 2 reports 15 representative proprietary and openly accessible models together with average tool calls and token usage. The testing efﬁciency of TOBench is elaborated in Appendix B.2.

sections.title: 5.2 Main Results
sections.content: Table 2 shows that TOBench is challenging for all tested models. The strongest model, Qwen3.5Plus, reaches only 41.0% average task success, while the best proprietary result is 32.0%. Difﬁculty is the dominant factor: performance is unsaturated even on easy tasks and collapses on hard splits, where the best scores are 20.00% on Customer Service-Hard and 15.38% on Intelligent CreationHard. The two macro families stress different capabilities: Customer Service rewards grounded retrieval and faithful tool use, whereas Intelligent Creation is especially sensitive to multimodal editing and ﬁnal-result veriﬁcation. sections.title: 5.3 Error Analysis
sections.content: To understand why performance remains low, we manually organize benchmark failures into ﬁve top-level categories: Tool Call Error, Tool Parameter Error, Multimodal Capability Deﬁcit, Self-Veriﬁcation Failure, and Non-Agent Error. Appendix summarizes the full taxonomy and subcategories used in our analysis. Tool call and parameter errors remain the most pervasive execution bottleneck. Many trajectories fail before high-level reasoning becomes relevant: models choose the wrong tool, omit required operation, hallucinate unsupported actions, or pass invalid arguments. These failures show that realistic MCP environments demand stronger tool-grounded action modeling than simpliﬁed function-calling benchmarks. Multimodal reasoning errors become dominant once basic execution succeeds. When models reach the correct tool family, failures often shift to perception and cross-modal inference, including ﬁne-grained visual extraction, spatial reasoning, temporal localization, and evidence alignment across modalities. More detailed bad cases and analysis for this category are provided in Appendix E.1. Figure 5: Heatmap of model-speciﬁc error counts across ﬁne-grained subcategories, grouped into tool call, tool parameter, multimodal ability, and self-veriﬁcation failures. 8 Figure 6: Two examples of failed tasks with initial and ﬁnal states are presented: the ﬁrst agent skipped visual validation of the generated PPT, leading to layout overlap and formatting errors, while the second failed to align the ASR transcription with the original video, resulting in incorrect frame evidence. The complete tool execution trajectories for both tasks are provided in Appendix E.2. Missing visual veriﬁcation is harness-speciﬁc failure mode. In many image editing, PPT authoring, and visual-generation tasks, models perform plausible edit and stop without inspecting the rendered result, or rely on metadata checks instead of true visual veriﬁcation. This directly explains why Intelligent Creation-Hard remains difﬁcult: the harness penalizes open-loop completion and rewards closed-loop self-correction. The error heatmap suggests distinct failure regimes across model tiers. Stronger models reduce low-level schema mistakes, but their remaining errors concentrate in multimodal reasoning and ﬁnal veriﬁcation; weaker models accumulate both low-level tool failures and high-level perception errors. Overall, many current agents still struggle to operate inside realistic evaluation harness even when they can produce superﬁcially plausible ﬁrst-pass outputs.

sections.title: 6 Limitations and Discussion
sections.content: Current scope. Although TOBench covers 100 executable tasks across 20 subcategory slices, it still covers only subset of real-world omni-modal work. Evaluator noise. The mixed evaluation strategy is necessary but imperfect: code-based checks can miss subtle failures, and multimodal judges may introduce bias. MCP dependence. The MCP-centric design improves realism for current ecosystems, but benchmark behavior may depend on evolving server descriptions, response formats, and implementation details. Benchmark realism versus controllability. central trade-off is realism versus reproducibility: realistic software stacks and multimodal assets better reﬂect user workloads, but they also increase evaluation complexity and variance.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: We introduced TOBench, benchmark and evaluation harness for omni-modal tool-using agents that emphasizes realistic professional workﬂows, MCP-native tool ecosystems, and closed-loop veriﬁcation over multimodal intermediate artifacts. Experiments on 15 contemporary models show that current systems remain far from robust omni-modal task completion: the best model reaches 41.0% overall success, hard tasks remain largely unsolved, and failures cluster around tool execution, multimodal reasoning, and missing veriﬁcation loops. We hope TOBench serves as practical testbed for studying how agents perceive, act, verify, and self-correct in realistic omni-modal environments.

sections.title: A Catalog of MCP Servers
sections.content: We show all the MCP servers used in the TOBench in Table 3. Most of these MCP servers are sourced from existing community-developed projects, providing broad foundation of realistic services for the benchmark. For substantial proportion of these community resources, we have implemented standardized refactoring. This includes not only optimizing tool output formats for but also strengthening error handling to ensure that agents receive more instructional feedback during long-horizon tasks. To support the core perceiveactinspectrevise loop, we developed several MCP servers for vision and audio modalities. We constructed the Image-Processing-Toolkits, which provides functions like cropping, pixelation, and watermarking, and integrates rendering engines to export PPTX, Excel, and Word documents as images. This enables agents to visually inspect layout and formatting for autonomous self-correction. Furthermore, we built the Image_Generation_Server powered by Seedream 4.0 to support generative tasks. For multimedia workﬂows, we introduced the video_audio_editor_toolkits, tool for omni-modal agents that enables precise editing of video clips and audio tracks. This allows agents to perform temporal-modal operations. For broader audio interaction, we also integrated Whisper (ASR) and Microsoft Edge TTS to bridge the gap in current native omni-modal models. We will make these optimized and newly developed MCP servers publicly available to the community.

sections.title: B Experimental Details of TOBench
sections.content: B.1 Deﬁnition of Benchmark Traits To provide clear understanding of the benchmark comparisons in Table 1, we deﬁne each column header as follows: Tasks refers to the total number of evaluation instances, while Dom. and Tools represent the count of distinct application domains and available external APIs, respectively. MCP Eco. indicates if the tool environment integrates the Model Context Protocol ecosystem, and Cross Scen. denotes whether tasks require operating across multiple scenarios in single workﬂow. Real Env. signiﬁes that observations derive from real-world applications with realistic state initialization, and Info. Grnd. ensures actions are supported by external tool evidence rather than internal parametric knowledge. Fuzzy Prompt means requests are natural and potentially under-speciﬁed without predeﬁned trajectories. Regarding modalities, Vis. and Aud. represent the need to process vision and audio information, Cross Modal denotes the seamless integration of multiple modalities simultaneously, and Gen. indicates tasks requiring the invocation of multimodal generation models to synthesize new artifacts. B.2 Execution Efﬁciency and Runtime The TOBench harness supports both sequential and parallel task execution. For our evaluation, we conducted tests on an AWS instance equipped with 4-core CPU and 16GB RAM, utilizing parallelism factor of 2. Under this conﬁguration, the total execution time for the agent to complete all tasks ranges from 3 to 8 hours. The actual evaluation efﬁciency depends on factors such as model reasoning complexity, the underlying response latency of MCP servers, and the speciﬁc parallelism conﬁguration.

sections.title: C Agent Context and Interaction Framework
sections.content: In TOBench, tasks are executed via dialogues between an agent and simulated user, governed by two independent system prompt architectures characterized by strategic information gap. Currently, the benchmark focuses on single-turn tasks, where the complete request is delivered in single interaction. The Agent System Prompt deﬁnes the ReAct reasoning framework and incorporates task-speciﬁc Domain Rules. These rules simulate professional standards and platform constraints (e.g., speciﬁc formatting styles or quality self-correction protocols) that are transparent to the user, requiring the agent to autonomously align with implicit professional requirements without direct guidance. Conversely, the User System Prompt ensures the simulated user acts as the single source of truth. It 13 Table 3: MCP servers currently integrated into TOBench. Instructions, ensuring complete information transfer while strictly prohibiting the disclosure of task solutions or misleading cues to maintain rigorous and fair evaluation. We use the Customer_Service/Hotel_Welcome_PPTX task as representative example to illustrate the speciﬁc context conﬁguration below. 14 Agent System Prompt ## Role You are an intelligent Omni-Modality ReAct (Reasoning and Acting) Agent that solves user tasks by role-playing speciﬁed persona and strictly following that personas Domain Rules. Your primary goal is to accurately solve user tasks by engaging in cycle of reasoning, planning, tool execution, and reﬂection. You must dynamically adapt your actions based on tool feedback to ensure high-quality outcomes. ### Persona Creative Experience Designer ### Domain Rules 1. Visual Consistency: When designing the hotels welcome-themed PPTX, you should ﬁrst review the template ﬁle example.pptx and then design the PPTX according to the templates layout. After completing the PPT, you should check that the layout is consistent and the design is visually appealing, and adjust the PPT as needed. 2. If the generated image does not meet the users actual requirements, you need to re-generate the image until it meets the users requirements. 3. PPTx details: The text for the Welcome Title should be set to 32 pt. The Greeting section text should be set to 24 pt. The provided hotel_logo.png must be inserted at the bottom of the slide, and the image should not be overly eye-catching. ## Operational Guidelines 1. Task Processing Protocol Analyze & Plan: Upon receiving request, explicitly reason about the requirements and formulate structured preliminary plan. Iterative Execution: Execute your plan step-by-step. After each tool call, analyze the result to decide the next step. If step fails or produces unexpected results, reﬂect on the cause and adjust your plan accordingly. Outcome Veriﬁcation: Merely executing tool calls is insufﬁcient. You must verify that the actual content generated or retrieved (e.g. cropped image, clipped video) strictly meets the users requirements before considering step complete. 2. Multimodal Data Handling Selective Inspection: You have access to multimodal inputs (text, images, audio, video). Inspect these assets only when essential for task comprehension or result veriﬁcation using available viewer tools. Document Standards: For document generation tasks (PPT, LaTeX, Word), you are responsible for ensuring professional formatting, layout consistency, and visual appeal. 3. Tool Usage & File Management Parameter Alignment: When using generation tools (e.g., for media creation), carefully select parameters that align with the speciﬁc context, style, and constraints of the users request. Absolute Paths Mandatory: You must use absolute paths for all ﬁle references, whether writing input_file_path or output_file_path params for tools. Relative paths are strictly prohibited. ## Critical Constraints Workspace Boundary: All ﬁle operations must occur within the accessible workspace directory: !!workspace_dir!!. Output ﬁles must be saved here. Task Termination: If the user sends the signal "#### STOP", or if you have self-veriﬁed that the task is fully completed, you must conclude the session. To ﬁnish, either call the local-claim_done tool (if available) or respond with ﬁnal text message without any tool calls. This action is irreversible. 15 User System Prompt # Role Setting You represent user interacting with an intelligent agent. You must strictly role-play the speciﬁc User Persona deﬁned below. Your goal is to convey the task to the agent through dialogue based on the content in instructions. Core Principle: All your expressions and demands must be: 1. Faithful to the Persona: Speak and act according to the identity, tone, and context deﬁned in User Persona. 2. Faithful to the Instructions: Your requests must strictly align with the Task Configuration. Do not hallucinate or deviate from the given task details. ## Task Conﬁguration ### User Persona am the General Manager of Nebula Heights Resort. ### Instructions We have very important guest checking in today: boy named Leo who is celebrating his 10th birthday and loves astronauts and space exploration. First, would like you to use the photo from Leos parents, Leo.png, to generate an image of Leo wearing spacesuit, standing on the moon, with spaceship next to him and blue starry sky as the background. This image should be named Leo_astronaut.png. Then, to welcome Leo, need you to create welcome_tv.pptx for the in-room TV. The PPTs Welcome Title should be Space Explorer, and the image you just created should be inserted into the position speciﬁed by the template. The Greeting section should say Happy 10th Birthday, Astronaut Leo!. Finally, please insert the provided hotel_logo.png into the PPTX. ### Turn Mode single_turn ## Guidelines ### Conversation Style Rules Context & Needs Combination: When making statements, adopt the Background Description + Speciﬁc Request pattern. First explain the background situation, then propose the speciﬁc task request. Statement Pacing: In single-turn scenarios, please state all content completely at once. In multi-turn scenarios, autonomously break down the task and state it gradually across multiple rounds. Do not dump all information at once. Response Principles: When the agent asks for information, provide only the conditions and preferences explicitly stated in instructions. Strictly prohibit assumptions, expansions, substitutions, or generalizations. ### Information Disclosure Rules Faithful & Natural: Directly convey the core information from instructions, but ﬁne-tune the expression based on the persona context to make it sound natural. Full Detail Coverage: You must ensure every detail in instructions is mentioned in the conversation (even seemingly trivial background information), as this information may affect the agents recommendations and decisions. ### Information Processing Rules Fact-Based Answers: When the agent asks for information, answer strictly based on instructions. If the information is not provided in instructions, explicitly answer dont remember or dont know. Never fabricate information not provided. Strict Adherence to Needs: Strictly follow the requirements stated in instructions when proposing needs. Resistance to Persuasion: When the agent tries to persuade you to change your needs, you must stick to the original requirements in instructions and do not compromise easily.

sections.title: D Detailed Error Taxonomy
sections.content: Table 4: Detailed error taxonomy used in TOBench analysis. The main paper discusses the ﬁve toplevel categories, while this appendix enumerates the ﬁner-grained subcategories used for trajectory annotation and error heatmaps. Category Subcategory Deﬁnition and Representative Symptoms Tool Call Error Tool Parameter Error Wrong Tool Selection Missing Essential Tool Tool Planning Error Hallucinated Tool Parameter Type Error Parameter Range Error Parameter Object Selection Error Missing Parameter Hallucinated Parameter Fine-grained Perception Multimodal Capability Deﬁcit Visual-Spatial Understanding Video Temporal Relationship Understanding Cross-modal Fusion Information Multimodal Hallucination Self-Veriﬁcation Failure No Veriﬁcation Pseudo-Veriﬁcation Incomplete Veriﬁcation Non-Agent Error False Positive on Error Trajectory MCP Issue Agent Base Model Issue The agent chooses tool whose functionality does not match the required operation, such as reading PDFs with plain-text tools or using ﬁlesystem writes to fake Ofﬁce artifacts. The workﬂow omits required tool invocation or necessary concluding step, such as skipping search for time-sensitive facts or forgetting save/export action. The agent uses tools in an inefﬁcient, illogical, or non-adaptive way, for example by repeating avoidable tool calls, oversampling videos frame-by-frame, or persisting with the same failed plan after an error. The agent invokes tool that does not exist in the available MCP environment. The agent chooses the correct tool but passes values with invalid data types or basic numeric validity, such as ﬂoats where integers are required or nonpositive intervals for frame extraction. The parameter value exceeds objective bounds of the target ﬁle, page range, or media length, such as requesting PDF pages beyond the document length. The parameter points to the wrong target object, URL, path, slide, worksheet, or region, such as broken URLs, nonexistent image paths, or editing the wrong worksheet. Required tool arguments are omitted, such as missing content ﬁelds or mandatory identiﬁers. The agent invents unsupported argument names or unsupported option values, such as invalid operation enums or nonexistent transition settings. The agent sees the asset but fails to extract crucial small details or structured information, such as misreading invoice ﬁelds, tables, or screenshots. The agent cannot reason accurately about coordinates, geometry, or layout constraints, leading to wrong crops, wrong mosaic targets, overlapping PPT elements, or boundary violations. The agent fails to localize or causally interpret time-dependent events in video or audio streams, such as choosing the wrong second for an event or missing causally important actions between sampled frames. The agent fails to align and jointly reason across text, image, audio, video, or document evidence, such as misaligning spoken descriptions with video timeline or failing multi-hop reasoning across text and map. Instead of admitting uncertainty or calling another tool, the agent fabricates visual or cross-modal content, such as inventing product defects, invoice amounts, or scene descriptions from blurry evidence. The agent edits or generates visual artifact and declares success without any visual inspection. The agent performs only non-visual checks that cannot validate the actual output quality, such as checking ﬁle existence, dimensions, or metadata instead of previewing the artifact. The agent veriﬁes early or local steps but skips ﬁnal global inspection after composition, such as inspecting source images but not the ﬁnal PPT page. The log contains the word error although the tool call itself is not actually an agent mistake, such as success payloads with error: null or ﬁlenames that naturally contain error. The failure is caused by the MCP implementation, provider, or surrounding infrastructure rather than by the agent decision, such as external API outages or MCP-side parsing issues. The base model fails due to system-level limitations rather than task-speciﬁc reasoning mistakes, such as context-window overﬂow on large multimodal inputs. Table 5: Taxonomy of task-speciﬁc evaluation points in TOBench. Different tasks mix these criterion types in different ways, which motivates per-task evaluator synthesis rather than single benchmark-wide scoring template. Criterion Type Format Constraints What It Checks Typical Targets Typical Veriﬁcation File names, storage paths, worksheet names, schema ﬁelds, document sections, required output formats. Layout ﬁdelity, required visual evidence, semantic content consistency, object presence, rendered document quality under task-deﬁned constraints. Required MCP invocation, search-grounded facts, map or weather results, externally updated values, tool-derived ﬁelds in generated ﬁles. Code-based checks over ﬁle existence, schema compliance, structural parsing, and exact ﬁeld matching. VLM-based judging after appropriate preprocessing such as rendering documents, parsing tables, or converting images to model-readable inputs. Tool-log veriﬁcation and, when needed, re-querying external tools or MCP servers at evaluation time to validate grounded results. Deterministic structural requirements explicitly speciﬁed by the task. Judge-Based Multimodal Constraints Semantic or visual Tool/Result Constraints properties that cannot be robustly reduced to exact string matching. Grounding requirements tied to tool usage or live external information rather than static reference ﬁles alone.

sections.title: Task Context
sections.content: User Request: have rough ﬂoor plan for the Johnson-Smith wedding in layout_sketch.jpg. Because the planning was so rushed, one small table and its chairs in the drawing edge was cancelled, and marked it with pencil as CANCELED. Please exclude this table and its chairs from all subsequent budget calculations. Based on your hotels standard table and chair pricing, please create an Excel ﬁle named wedding_budget.xlsx. In the Excel sheet, list the items (tables and chairs), quantities, unit prices, and total costs. Be sure to use actual Excel formulas for the calculations so can update it later. Domain Rules: Senior Event Coordinator 1. Inventory & Pricing: The hotels standard rates are $25 for round tables, $30 for rectangular tables, and $5 per chair. 2. Excel Format: The Excel workbook should contain only one worksheet named budget, and all calculations and summaries must be performed on this sheet. Figure 7: Rough ﬂoor plan for the Johnson-Smith wedding layout_sketch.jpg. (a) (b) Figure 8: Comparison of output results: (a) Ground truth for the signature placement task; (b) Final image generated by Gemini 3 Pro. In the task Customer_Service/Hotel_Wedding_Layout, agent (Gemini 3 Pro) needs to count the types and quantities of tables and chairs required for the wedding based on the wedding seating sketch provided by the user. It shall then ﬁll in the unit prices of seats speciﬁed in the hotels domain rules into an Excel sheet, and use Excel formulas to calculate the ﬁnal results. During the execution process, the agent successfully viewed the layout sketch image but failed to accurately count the number of chairs to be included in the budget. It wrote 28 chairs into the Excel ﬁle, whereas the ground truth was 27. 19 E.1.2 Visual-Spatial Understanding

sections.title: Task Context
sections.content: User Request: am rental agency intermediary and need to place two electronic signatures onto the correct positions in lease agreement image. Place landlord.png onto the LANDLORDS SIGNATURE line at the bottom of rental_agreement.jpg, and place tenant.png onto the TENANTS SIGNATURE line. Output ﬁnalized signed contract image. Domain Rules: 1. Output Scope: Only generate the ﬁnal composited image ﬁle. Do not create Word, PDF, or other document formats. 2. Placement Requirement: The landlords signature must be placed on the line labeled LANDLORDS SIGNATURE at the bottom of the contract. The tenants signature must be placed on the line labeled TENANTS SIGNATURE. 3. Size and Proportion: Each signature must be proportionally scaled according to the length of the signature line, preserving the original aspect ratio without stretching. Signature strokes must remain clear and legible. 4. No Obstruction: The signatures must not cover the labels (LANDLORDS SIGNATURE / TENANTS SIGNATURE) or any critical contract text. They should only overlap the signature line area. 5. Output Naming: Save the ﬁnal output ﬁle as signed_rental_agreement.png. (a) (b) Figure 9: Initial and output ﬁles for the signature placement task: (a) Original lease agreement image (rental_agreement.jpg); (b) Output generated by Qwen 3.5 Plus showing spatial misalignment. In the task Customer_Service/Real_Estate-rental_agreement, agent (Qwen 3.5 Plus) needs to use visual recognition to precisely locate the signature labels and lines at the bottom of the agreement within the source image. Subsequently, the agent must dynamically scale the signature images based on the line length and transparently composite them onto speciﬁc pixel coordinates while ensuring no contract text is obstructed. During the execution process, the agent continuously attempted to adjust the placement and scaling of the images from turn 5 to turn 40. However, the ﬁnal results show that the landlords signature overlaps the text labels, while the tenants signature severely deviates from the designated area and even obscures the footer. Furthermore, the signature dimensions are oversized, which directly violates domain rules regarding proportional scaling and positional alignment. 21 E.1.3 Video Temporal Relationship Understanding

sections.title: Task Context
sections.content: User Request: My name is Alex. encountered very aggressive food delivery courier. My order number is 35536. During the delivery, the courier smashed my food and even ate part of my order, causing me loss. have provided video as evidence and hope the platform can investigate this incident and offer appropriate compensation for my loss. Domain Rules: 1. Order and Courier Veriﬁcation: Based on the order number provided by the user, the agent must search the Excel order table to identify the corresponding courier information (such as name or courier ID). If the order cannot be found in the table, this must be explicitly stated in the report, and the agent must not speculate about the couriers identity. 2. Video Evidence Review Principle: All judgments regarding the couriers behavior (including but not limited to damaging food, consuming customer items, or engaging in conﬂict) must be strictly based on what is directly observable in the video. The agent must not infer actions that are not clearly shown. If key details are unclear or not visible, the report must clearly state that the behavior cannot be conﬁrmed. 3. Report Output Requirement: The agent must generate formal investigation report in Word format. The ﬁle name should follow generic incident-based naming convention and must not hard-code the order number. 4. Report Structure Speciﬁcation: The report must contain the following top-level sections in order: (1) Incident Summary (2) Courier Information (sourced from order data) (3) Video Evidence Observations (objective, factual description only) (4) Conﬂict Cause Analysis (5) Handling Recommendations and Conclusion. 5.
